text: The surgeon mobilizes the distal left ureter, using a hemlock clip attached to a suture to assist in flipping the ureter. Frozen sections of the ureter are sent for intraoperative pathologic analysis. The posterior prostate is mobilized through Denonvilliers' fascia, and the right ureter is clipped similarly to the left.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection towards the area of the right distal ureter, mobilizing the lateral edge of the bladder by sweeping it off the pelvic sidewall and iliac vessels. The lateral dissection exposes the posterior pedicle, which is divided using a vessel seal device. The obturator nerve is visible, ensuring it is protected from injury.

The surgeon incises the endopelvic fascia on both sides of the prostate. Nerve sparing is performed in an anti-grade fashion. The space of Retzius is developed, and apical dissection of the prostate is achieved by separating the levator fibers and the dorsal venous complex from the prostate. A dorsal venous fixation stitch is placed to ensure hemostasis and aid in continence preservation.

The surgeon separates the dorsal venous complex and the recto urethralis muscle from the urethra. The distal urethra is clipped and divided, with frozen sections sent for pathologic analysis. Remaining attachments are dissected in a retroapical fashion, and the specimen is placed into a fifteen millimeter endocatch bag for retrieval.

Pelvic lymphadenectomy as well as intracorporeal ileal loop diversion are performed in the standard fashion as described and published elsewhere. The patient did well postoperatively and was discharged home on postoperative day four. He currently has no evidence of disease recurrence.
